news_summary: Today's news highlights human resilience and ingenuity, from surviving ancient supervolcano disasters to fostering community openness and inventing essential tools.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en lumière la résilience et l'ingéniosité humaines, qu'il s'agisse de survivre aux catastrophes causées par d'anciens supervolcans, de favoriser l'ouverture au sein des communautés ou d'inventer des outils indispensables.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy ponen de relieve la resiliencia y el ingenio humanos, desde la supervivencia a catástrofes provocadas por antiguos supervolcanes hasta el fomento de la apertura de la comunidad y la invención de herramientas esenciales.

articles.title: A supervolcano nearly wiped out humanity 74,000 years ago, but humans did something incredible
articles.summary: The Toba supereruption 74,000 years ago was so massive it may have plunged Earth into years of darkness and cold, leading some scientists to believe humanity nearly went extinct. Yet archaeological evidence from Africa and Asia suggests early humans were far more resilient than once thought. Instead of disappearing, some communities adapted with new tools, new survival strategies, and remarkable flexibility. The disaster may not have destroyed humanity — it may have revealed just how tough humans really are.
articles.content:
Seventy four thousand years ago, Earth experienced one of the most powerful volcanic disasters in the past 2.5 million years. The Toba supereruption, centered in what is now Indonesia, unleashed devastation on a staggering scale. The eruption was so enormous that scientists believe its effects reached nearly every corner of the planet.

For archaeologists who study ancient volcanic events, one question stands out above all others: How did humans survive? The Toba eruption was more than 10,000 times larger than the 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens, yet our species endured.

The Toba Supereruption Changed the Planet

The eruption blasted 672 cubic miles (2,800 km3) of volcanic ash high into the stratosphere and left behind a gigantic crater measuring about (62 x 18 miles, or 100 x 30 kilometers). The skies may have darkened for years as volcanic material blocked sunlight around the globe, triggering severe cooling.

Closer to the eruption, conditions would have been catastrophic. Acid rain likely polluted drinking water, while thick ash buried vegetation and wildlife beneath heavy layers of debris.

Given those extreme conditions, it is remarkable that Homo sapiens survived at all.

Did Humanity Nearly Go Extinct?

People living closest to the volcano were probably wiped out completely. Scientists are still investigating how much the eruption affected humans living farther away.

For many years, researchers supported what became known as the Toba catastrophe hypothesis. According to this idea, the eruption caused a volcanic winter that lasted as long as six years and pushed the global human population down to fewer than 10,000 people.

Part of the evidence comes from human DNA. Genetic studies suggest modern humans spread into different parts of the world roughly 100,000 years ago and later experienced what scientists call a genetic bottleneck. A bottleneck happens when a disaster or disease sharply reduces population numbers, leaving behind far less genetic diversity.

Researchers continue to debate whether the Toba eruption caused this population decline or whether another factor was responsible. Climate records, environmental evidence and archaeological discoveries are helping scientists piece together what really happened.

Tiny Volcanic Glass Reveals Ancient Clues

One of the most important tools for studying the eruption is the volcanic material it left behind. Scientists call this material tephra, and they can trace its spread across landscapes using both visual and chemical analysis.

Some of the smallest fragments, known as cryptotephra, travel the farthest distances. These microscopic pieces of volcanic glass are invisible to the naked eye, making them extremely difficult to find.

Researchers carefully sift through dirt samples and use specialized tools called micromanipulators to isolate the tiny shards. The process can take months for a single archaeological site and often feels like searching for a needle in a haystack.

Each volcanic eruption produces tephra with a distinct chemical signature. Some samples contain more iron or different mineral combinations than others. By studying these chemical fingerprints, scientists can determine exactly which eruption produced a specific layer of ash.

In the field, archaeologists search for cryptotephra at sites containing evidence of ancient human activity, including tools, artwork and buried remains. After collecting samples, researchers bring them back to the lab, separate the microscopic glass from the soil and chemically analyze it to confirm whether it came from the Toba eruption.

How Archaeologists Track Human Survival

Finding Toba ash at an archaeological site is only the first step. Scientists then compare evidence of human activity from before and after the eruption.

Sometimes people appear to change how they lived after a volcanic disaster. They may begin using new stone tool technologies, switch food sources or adapt to changing environments. In other cases, sites are completely abandoned.

Climate and environmental records add another layer to the story by showing how temperatures, rainfall and vegetation changed after the eruption. Together, these clues help researchers understand how ancient humans responded to extreme environmental stress.

Evidence Shows Humans Adapted

Despite the scale of the disaster, many archaeological sites point to human resilience rather than collapse.

At Pinnacle Point 5-6 in South Africa, researchers found evidence of Toba cryptotephra in layers showing continuous human occupation before, during and after the eruption. Human activity at the site actually increased afterward, along with signs of new technological innovations.

Similar discoveries have been made at Shinfa-Metema 1 in Ethiopia. There, cryptotephra from Toba appears in layers that also contain evidence of ongoing human activity.

Researchers found that people adapted by following seasonal rivers and fishing in shallow waterholes during long dry periods. Around the same time, humans in the region also adopted bow and arrow technology. This flexibility may have helped them survive harsh environmental conditions linked to the eruption.

Comparable evidence has also emerged from archaeological sites in Indonesia, India and China. As more discoveries accumulate, many scientists now believe humans were far more adaptable than the original Toba catastrophe hypothesis suggested.

The eruption may not have caused the dramatic population collapse once proposed, but it still offers valuable insight into how humans respond to extreme disasters.

What Toba Can Teach Us Today

Humans today are far better prepared for volcanic disasters than people were 74,000 years ago. Modern monitoring programs, including the USGS Volcanic Hazards Program and the Global Volcanism Program, track active volcanoes using advanced technology and provide warnings about eruptions around the world.

Still, one lesson from Toba stands out clearly. Human survival has always depended on adaptability. By studying how ancient people responded to catastrophic eruptions, scientists hope to better understand which survival strategies mattered most and how those lessons could help us face future disasters.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the remarkable resilience and adaptability of early humans in surviving one of Earth's largest volcanic disasters, the Toba supereruption 74,000 years ago. This real historical event has broad significance for understanding human survival, adaptability, and responses to extreme environmental challenges. The detailed archaeological and scientific evidence provides substantial context and insight into human history and survival strategies, offering hope and inspiration about human endurance and adaptability.
articles.personality_title: Early humans survived a massive supervolcano 74,000 years ago by adapting
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Around 74,000 years ago, a huge volcanic eruption called the Toba supereruption happened in what is now Indonesia. This eruption was one of the largest in millions of years and caused darkness and cold around the world.

**What happened** – The Toba eruption sent a massive amount of ash into the sky, blocking sunlight and making the Earth much colder for years. Nearby plants and animals were buried under thick ash, and water was polluted. Despite this, humans living far from the volcano did not disappear. Archaeologists found evidence that people in Africa, Asia, and Indonesia kept living, made new tools, and changed how they hunted and gathered food.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that early humans were very tough and flexible. Instead of dying out, they learned to survive in harsh conditions by changing their ways. For example, in Ethiopia, people started using bows and arrows and fished in new places to find food. This challenges earlier ideas that almost all humans died after the eruption.

**What's next step** – Scientists will keep studying tiny volcanic ash pieces called cryptotephra found at ancient sites to learn more about how humans lived through this event. Understanding these survival strategies can help us prepare better for future natural disasters.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Toba supereruption nearly destroyed life, but early humans survived by adapting their tools and behaviors to new challenges, showing remarkable resilience.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les premiers humains ont survécu à un supervolcan il y a 74 000 ans en s’adaptant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a environ 74 000 ans, une énorme éruption volcanique appelée la superéruption de Toba a eu lieu dans ce qui est aujourd’hui l’Indonésie. Cette éruption fut l’une des plus grandes depuis des millions d’années et a provoqué de l’obscurité et du froid dans le monde entier.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’éruption de Toba a envoyé une immense quantité de cendres dans le ciel, bloquant la lumière du soleil et rendant la Terre beaucoup plus froide pendant plusieurs années. Les plantes et les animaux proches ont été ensevelis sous une épaisse couche de cendres, et l’eau a été polluée. Malgré cela, les humains vivant loin du volcan n’ont pas disparu. Les archéologues ont trouvé des preuves que des populations en Afrique, en Asie et en Indonésie ont continué à vivre, fabriqué de nouveaux outils, et changé leurs façons de chasser et de se nourrir.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les premiers humains étaient très résistants et flexibles. Au lieu de mourir, ils ont appris à survivre dans des conditions difficiles en adaptant leurs habitudes. Par exemple, en Éthiopie, les gens ont commencé à utiliser des arcs et des flèches et à pêcher dans de nouveaux endroits. Cela remet en question l’idée qu’ils ont presque tous disparu après l’éruption.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier les petites cendres volcaniques appelées cryptotéphra trouvées sur les sites anciens pour en savoir plus sur la vie des humains à cette époque. Comprendre ces stratégies de survie peut nous aider à mieux nous préparer aux futures catastrophes naturelles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La superéruption de Toba a failli détruire la vie, mais les premiers humains ont survécu en adaptant leurs outils et comportements, montrant une grande résilience.
articles.personality_title_es: Los primeros humanos sobrevivieron a un supervolcán hace 74,000 años adaptándose
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace unos 74,000 años, ocurrió una enorme erupción volcánica llamada la supererupción de Toba en lo que hoy es Indonesia. Esta erupción fue una de las más grandes en millones de años y causó oscuridad y frío en todo el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – La erupción de Toba lanzó una gran cantidad de ceniza al cielo, bloqueando la luz del sol y enfriando mucho la Tierra durante años. Las plantas y animales cercanos quedaron enterrados bajo capas gruesas de ceniza, y el agua se contaminó. Sin embargo, los humanos que vivían lejos del volcán no desaparecieron. Los arqueólogos encontraron pruebas de que personas en África, Asia e Indonesia continuaron viviendo, crearon nuevas herramientas y cambiaron la forma en que cazaban y obtenían alimentos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que los primeros humanos eran muy fuertes y flexibles. En lugar de morir, aprendieron a sobrevivir en condiciones difíciles adaptando sus costumbres. Por ejemplo, en Etiopía, las personas comenzaron a usar arcos y flechas y a pescar en nuevos lugares. Esto desafía la idea anterior de que casi todos murieron después de la erupción.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando pequeñas partículas de ceniza volcánica llamadas criptotephra encontradas en sitios antiguos para aprender más sobre cómo vivieron los humanos en ese tiempo. Entender estas estrategias de supervivencia puede ayudarnos a prepararnos mejor para futuros desastres naturales.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La supererupción de Toba casi destruye la vida, pero los primeros humanos sobrevivieron adaptando sus herramientas y comportamientos, demostrando una gran resistencia.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast volcanic crater emitting gentle ash clouds blending into a calm sky, with abstract silhouettes of ancient stone tools and delicate microscopic glass shards floating subtly above a flowing river, symbolizing human resilience and adaptation after the supereruption.

articles.title: What Hanoi learned by tearing down its park fences and opening up to everyone
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM In many cities, entering a park is a deliberate act. You adjust your route, find a gate, and cross from public pavement into a space that operates by its own rules, even if those rules are minimal. Hanoi is dismantling that dynamic. Across four major parks, including Cầu Giấy, […]
The post What Hanoi learned by tearing down its park fences and opening up to everyone first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

In many cities, entering a park is a deliberate act. You adjust your route, find a gate, and cross from public pavement into a space that operates by its own rules, even if those rules are minimal.

Hanoi is dismantling that dynamic. Across four major parks, including Cầu Giấy, Bách Thảo, Thống Nhất, and Indira Gandhi, the city has been removing the iron fences that long marked their edges. The gates are gone. The boundaries now blend into surrounding streets and sidewalks. You can enter from any direction, at any time, as part of a commute rather than a detour.

“Before, we had to walk far to get in through the main gate,” said Hoàng Thị Hiền, a resident of Thanh Xuân Ward. “Now I can enter the park from anywhere. It feels open and natural, like it’s part of my neighbourhood.”

The shift is aligned with Hanoi’s longer-range urban planning goals and reflects a rethink of what public space is supposed to do.

What open access actually changes

At Thống Nhất Park, one of the earliest sites in the program, more than two kilometres (about 1.2 miles) of fencing has been removed, connecting the park to the street grid around it. The effect on foot traffic was immediate.

“Now people can enter and exit from many directions, making access much more convenient and significantly increasing usage,” said Nguyễn Tiến Quang, vice chairman of Hai Bà Trưng Ward. “The park is no longer just a place to visit. It has become directly integrated into residents’ daily lives.”

That shift shows in who turns up and when. Early mornings and late afternoons now draw a broader cross-section of residents than the parks ever saw before, from elderly exercise groups to young families, with international visitors and students adding new layers of activity.

At Bách Thảo Park, the change has attracted a younger crowd. “We are seeing more young visitors coming to take photos, picnic and children playing in areas newly connected to sidewalks,” said Nguyễn Thị Lành, head of the park’s maintenance team, noting that the simultaneous removal of entrance fees made the change more accessible across income levels.

The psychology of a fence

Architect Phạm Anh Tuấn of Hà Nội University of Civil Engineering draws a distinction worth sitting with: physical access and felt access are not the same thing.

“Even before the fences were removed, access to parks was not particularly difficult,” he said. “However, in terms of spatial organization, removing the fences has created a much more open environment. More importantly, it brings a sense of closeness. Parks become a natural part of daily life.”

A fence, even an unlocked one, sends a message about separation. It marks the park as a space that operates on different terms, and entering it requires intention. Without that boundary, the park reads differently. At Indira Gandhi Park, that change in reading is reshaping perception of the surrounding area. “When the fences were removed, the park fully integrated with surrounding sidewalks and streets,” said Nguyễn Anh Dũng, vice chairman of Giảng Võ Ward. “The open views help reduce the sense of congestion from surrounding concrete blocks, creating a softer transition between urban spaces.”

Urban planners observe that green space is particularly effective at easing visual density in built environments. Without a fence framing it as a distinct object, a park becomes woven into the city’s fabric rather than set against it.

Openness brings new management demands

The transition is not frictionless. Higher foot traffic has introduced challenges around illegal parking and unauthorized vendors at some locations.

“In the past, management was concentrated at entry gates. With open parks, control must now extend across the entire space,” said Quang, whose ward responded by increasing patrols and reorganizing activity zones within the park.

Tuấn sees community ownership as the more durable answer over time. “Organizing cultural and artistic activities suited to each space will attract more people. When they participate, they not only use the space but also own it in a spiritual sense, which helps raise awareness of preservation,” he said.

The underlying logic is that people who feel ownership over a place tend to look after it. Whether that holds across Hanoi’s parks in the long run remains to be seen. What the early results show is that removing a barrier achieved something that urban planning often struggles to accomplish: it made people feel that a public space was genuinely theirs.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes Hanoi's removal of park fences to create open, accessible public green spaces integrated into daily urban life. This urban planning initiative has broad positive social impact by increasing park usage across diverse groups, fostering community ownership, and improving the urban environment's accessibility and inclusivity. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful change benefiting the general public.
articles.personality_title: Hanoi removes park fences to welcome everyone and connect neighborhoods
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many cities have parks surrounded by fences and gates, which make entering a park a special, planned action. These fences create a clear border between the city streets and the green space inside.

**What happened** – Hanoi city decided to take down the fences around four major parks: Cầu Giấy, Bách Thảo, Thống Nhất, and Indira Gandhi. This change allows people to enter the parks from any direction at any time, making the parks feel like a natural part of the neighborhood instead of separate places.

**Impact** – Removing fences has made parks easier to access and more inviting. More people now use these parks daily, including elderly groups, families, young people, students, and visitors. The parks have become places where people spend time during their regular routines, not just special visits. This change also helps parks blend into the city better, making the surroundings feel less crowded and more open.

**What's next step** – While more visitors bring benefits, they also cause challenges like illegal parking and unplanned vendors. City workers are adjusting by increasing patrols and organizing park spaces. Experts suggest that involving the community through cultural and artistic activities will help people feel connected and responsible for the parks, encouraging them to take care of these open spaces.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By removing fences, Hanoi has transformed its parks into open, welcoming spaces that connect communities and become part of everyday life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Hanoï enlève les clôtures de ses parcs pour mieux connecter les quartiers
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans beaucoup de villes, les parcs sont entourés de clôtures et de portails, ce qui fait que l'entrée dans un parc est un acte réfléchi. Ces clôtures créent une limite claire entre la rue et l’espace vert.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La ville de Hanoï a décidé d’enlever les clôtures autour de quatre grands parcs : Cầu Giấy, Bách Thảo, Thống Nhất et Indira Gandhi. Cette modification permet aux habitants d’entrer dans les parcs de n’importe quel côté et à tout moment, rendant les parcs plus naturels et intégrés au quartier.

**Impact** – La suppression des clôtures facilite l’accès et rend les parcs plus accueillants. Plus de personnes utilisent ces espaces chaque jour, notamment des groupes de personnes âgées, des familles, des jeunes, des étudiants et des visiteurs. Les parcs sont devenus des lieux de vie quotidiens, pas seulement des destinations spéciales. Ce changement aide aussi les parcs à mieux s’intégrer dans la ville, rendant les environs moins étouffants et plus ouverts.

**Prochaine étape** – L’augmentation des visiteurs apporte aussi des défis comme le stationnement illégal et les vendeurs non autorisés. Les autorités renforcent la surveillance et organisent mieux les espaces. Les experts pensent que des activités culturelles et artistiques impliquant la communauté aideront les habitants à se sentir responsables et à mieux protéger ces espaces ouverts.

**Une phrase clé** – En supprimant les clôtures, Hanoï a transformé ses parcs en espaces ouverts et accueillants qui relient les communautés au quotidien.
articles.personality_title_es: Hanoi quita las rejas de sus parques para conectar mejor los barrios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En muchas ciudades, los parques están rodeados por rejas y puertas, lo que hace que entrar sea un acto pensado. Estas rejas marcan una frontera clara entre las calles y los espacios verdes.

**Qué pasó** – La ciudad de Hanoi decidió quitar las rejas alrededor de cuatro parques importantes: Cầu Giấy, Bách Thảo, Thống Nhất e Indira Gandhi. Esto permite que las personas entren desde cualquier lado y en cualquier momento, haciendo que los parques se sientan como parte natural del barrio.

**Impacto** – Quitar las rejas ha facilitado el acceso y ha hecho que los parques sean más acogedores. Más gente usa estos parques todos los días, incluyendo grupos de personas mayores, familias, jóvenes, estudiantes y visitantes. Los parques se han convertido en lugares para la vida diaria, no solo para visitas especiales. Este cambio también ayuda a integrar mejor los parques en la ciudad, haciendo que el entorno se sienta menos congestionado y más abierto.

**Próximo paso** – Más visitantes también traen retos como el estacionamiento ilegal y vendedores no autorizados. Los trabajadores municipales están aumentando las patrullas y organizando mejor los espacios. Los expertos sugieren que actividades culturales y artísticas con la comunidad ayudarán a que las personas se sientan dueñas y cuiden mejor estos espacios abiertos.

**Una frase clave** – Al quitar las rejas, Hanoi ha convertido sus parques en espacios abiertos y acogedores que conectan a las comunidades en la vida diaria.
articles.image_prompt: An inviting, fence-free city park seamlessly blending into surrounding sidewalks and streets, with open pathways flowing naturally into the neighborhood; symbolic silhouettes of diverse groups—elderly exercising, young families picnicking, students with cameras—represented by abstract, colorful figures made of light and gentle brushstrokes, all under soft, natural hues conveying warmth and community integration.

articles.title: The story of the first human tool: the humble container
articles.summary: An analysis of ancient human artefacts finds that the container, a simple but critical tool, may have originated 500,000 years ago. Columnist Michael Marshall explores how slings, ostrich eggs and wooden trays helped our ancestors survive
articles.content:
An analysis of ancient human artefacts finds that the container, a simple but critical tool, may have originated 500,000 years ago. Columnist Michael Marshall explores how slings, ostrich eggs and wooden trays helped our ancestors survive

An oil lamp found in Lascaux cave in France Sémhur CC BY-SA 4.0

This is an extract from Our Human Story, our newsletter about the revolution in archaeology. Sign up to receive it in your inbox every month.

In the prehistoric opening of 2001: A Space Odyssey, the first tool is a club: a long limb bone that proves handy for killing prey animals, murdering the leader of a rival hominin group and, finally, for throwing into the air ready for a dramatic jump cut. In this view, the first tool was a weapon.

This interpretation can be neatly fitted onto the earliest stone tools. Rounded rocks were used for hitting things, sharp ones for cutting and stabbing. We imagine that early stone tools were used for breaking things open, for hunting, for killing.

However, early people probably used other kinds of tools as well, ones made of other materials. Plant materials like wood were probably used all the time; it’s just that they are less likely to be preserved. The Stone Age was also the Botanic Age (and if you haven’t read Sophie Berdugo’s 2024 feature on this, I urge you to do so).

This opens up a lot of other possibilities, one of the most thought-provoking being: containers. What if the first tool was an object that could hold something valuable, so you could carry it around or store it?

If you think about it, a container is one of the most useful things you can have. “It solves a lot of problems,” says palaeoanthropologist Marc Kissel at Appalachian State University in North Carolina. “It opens up this new niche.” He and his colleagues have compiled a database of prehistoric containers. There are hundreds of examples, spanning over 100,000 years, but they argue – I think rightly – that this is a tiny fraction of what once existed. Furthermore, the container was one of the most important tools. “It’s one of the things that allow humans to be so successful,” says Kissel.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Each month, Michael Marshall unearths the latest news and ideas about ancient humans, evolution, archaeology and more. Sign up to newsletter

Back in time

Creating a database of prehistoric containers isn’t easy. Kissel and his colleagues spent over a year scouring the scientific literature for examples. They couldn’t rely on the word “container” or a synonym turning up in the texts, so they had to look for a lot of other terms that represented specific types of container. They called a halt last year, realising that “we have to stop adding stuff”, he says, and recently published a paper describing the dataset in the Journal of Anthropological Archaeology on 8 April.

A further challenge was deciding what counts as a container. They chose a deliberately broad definition: “an object which fulfils the basic container principle (that of holding something inside themselves and acting as a barrier, separating that something from the external world) and is transportable via carriage on or by the human body”.

This has the effect of including a lot of objects that you might not, at first glance, think of as containers. One example is spoons, which we think of as utensils, but which hold something and allow you to transport it. There are also a lot of items interpreted as lamps. Each one is a small slab of rock with a divot carved out, into which people could place animal fats and burn them. The most famous example is from Lascaux Cave in France: it was carved from red sandstone and has a handle.

Other containers are made of hollow bones. For instance, tubes made from the wing bones of swans may have been used to carry needles. Ostrich eggs, which are large and sturdy, were used as containers in Africa, perhaps to carry water on long journeys. There are also some examples of rock art that show containers, such as an engraving from Gönnersdorf in Germany showing what looks like a net.

Engraved ostrich eggshells, likely fragments of ostrich eggshell containers from South Africa Texier et al. 2013

The team ended up with 793 mobile containers. While they tried to find containers from the entire Pleistocene, the period spanning 2.58 million to 11,700 years ago, all the examples they found seem to be from the last 500,000 years.

Nevertheless, this represents a big push into the deep past. Traditionally, archaeologists have thought of containers as arising only within the last 10,000 years or so. The idea was that they were tied to the advent of farming and settled living – the so-called Neolithic revolution – and the invention of pottery. Agricultural societies would produce food surpluses, which needed to be preserved and stored, whereas hunter-gatherers probably didn’t have surpluses, and in a highly mobile society pots would probably get broken anyway.

However, Kissel says this idea had largely been abandoned already, because it presents the Neolithic as a hard break with the past, when in reality it was much more gradual and piecemeal. In line with that, some Indigenous peoples in Australia made pottery more than 2000 years ago (though that gets us into the complicated question of whether they were hunter-gatherers, farmers or – probably more likely – something more complicated in between). Likewise, foragers that settled in the Amazon 10,000 years ago left behind shards of pottery, and there is evidence of pottery in China as early as 18,000 years ago.

This suggests that people developed containers gradually, building up more types over many years. “I think it’s more helpful to see containers on a spectrum,” says Kissel.

The origins of containers lie deep in the past. Perhaps not too far back, though, because non-human primates like great apes don’t use them. “They will occasionally take a leaf, dip the leaf into the water, and have it act as a sponge to get the water to their mouths,” says Kissel. “But they don’t have containers, and that, to me, seems like a foundational difference.”

The oldest container in the database is a tray or dish made of bark. It was found at Kalambo Falls in Zambia and is between 400,000 and 500,000 years old. Kalambo Falls has some incredible preserved wooden objects: in 2023, archaeologists described what seem to be large wooden structures, perhaps houses, from 476,000 years ago. However, the dating of the tray/dish is less clear.

This exemplifies another problem Kissel’s team had, which is that a lot of these objects were excavated a long time ago. This means the information about them is buried in old literature, which is often not online, and the dating methods used would not pass muster today. The tray from Kalambo Falls was excavated in the 1950s by a team led by archaeologist John Desmond Clark. The main source about the tray is Clark’s two-volume book Kalambo Falls Prehistoric Site (1969), which contains a three-page chapter by botanist Timothy Charles Whitmore on “bark and other specimens”. There’s also a 1958 article Clark wrote for Scientific American. That’s not a lot to go on.

Hence, while there are some trends in the dataset, Kissell says they should mostly be understood as reflecting the limitations of the archaeological record, rather than the facts of prehistory.

For instance, 87.8 per cent of the containers in the database were found in Europe. “I don’t think that is showing that Europe is the place where containers began,” says Kissel. Instead, it’s a reflection of the huge amount of archaeological work performed in Europe compared to elsewhere. Also, we just saw that the oldest container in the database is from Africa.

Likewise, of the containers that have dates at all, only two are older than 100,000 years. However, Kissel is explicit that hominins were probably using containers long before this: these objects either haven’t been preserved or haven’t been spotted. (Other researchers have made this same point.)

What we can start to see, Kissel argues, is how ubiquitous containers were. In Europe, which is well studied, there are lots of examples despite the preservation issues. That suggests they were crucial to human survival.

Storage, not wars

We’re still using one of the oldest tools humans invented Ron Giling/Alamy

One of the earliest uses of containers, Kissel argues, was to carry babies – perhaps in slings. A lot of anthropologists – especially female ones – have suggested this over the decades.

Among great apes like chimpanzees, the babies cling to their mothers, who are helpfully covered with thick fur that serves as a handhold. However, we have lost most of our body hair, and our newborns are so helpless that they can’t cling to anything.

With that in mind, Kissel suggests that baby carriers would have become useful when hominins started routinely walking on two legs, and when they lost most of their body hair. That happened several million years ago. “Australopithecines were probably using slings,” he says. If that’s true, then Lucy, the famous Australopithecus afarensis, was probably carried in a sling as a baby 3.4 million years ago.

One thing Kissel emphasised is that none of these ideas are new. People have been saying things like this for decades, but the ideas are only slowly moving to the forefront, perhaps because they were first articulated in feminist reinterpretations of prehistory, which were unfairly treated as kooky or unrealistic.

In 1976, anthropologists Nancy Tanner and Adrienne Zihlman proposed that some of the first tools might have been baskets, which women used to carry things like food. They were pushing back against male-dominated ideas of prehistory, which emphasised activities like hunting of large animals – incorrectly assumed to be primarily male-driven – and paid little or no attention to females.

Feminist journalist Elizabeth Fisher suggested much the same thing in her 1979 book Woman’s Creation: Sexual evolution and the shaping of society, in which she wrote that “Many theorizers feel that the earliest cultural inventions must have been a container to hold gathered products and some kind of sling or net carrier”.

Speculative fiction writer Ursula Le Guin cited Fisher directly in her essay The Carrier Bag Theory of Fiction. “If you haven’t got something to put it in, food will escape you – even something as uncombative and unresourceful as an oat,” she wrote. Even a simple container will allow you to gather a surplus, which means you can stay inside the next day if the weather is bad.

Le Guin pushed this idea a long way. She said that our ideas about history and prehistory are shaped by action and violence, like the first tool in 2001 and all the heroic stories about killing dragons and bad guys. But, she argued, there are other equally valid stories to tell about gathering and parenting and building.

Many of our stories are focused on “how Cain fell on Abel and how the bomb fell on Nagasaki and how the burning jelly fell on the villagers and how the missiles will fall on the Evil Empire, and all the other steps in the Ascent of Man,” Le Guin wrote. “Lest there be no more telling of stories at all, some of us out here in the wild oats, amid the alien corn, think we’d better start telling another one, which maybe people can go on with when the old one’s finished.”

As I write this, I can feel some of the reader letters being written: the ones that say violence has always been part of the human story and that when it comes to understanding prehistory, we should just follow the data.

The thing is, as best I can, I am following the data. There is growing evidence that the thing that makes our species unusual isn’t that we’re intelligent or creative or aggressive – though we undoubtedly can be all those things – but that we’re friendly and emotionally needy and dependent on each other. For instance, whenever a human population has become isolated from others, they have been at far greater risk of extinction; they had no one to ask for help.

Our survival depends on networking and cooperating. Like, say, sharing some of your stored food with a friend who has come up short, using that handy container you made.
articles.reasoning: The article presents a significant archaeological discovery that containers, a fundamental human tool, originated around 500,000 years ago, reshaping our understanding of human evolution and survival. This insight highlights the importance of cooperation, storage, and nurturing in human history, offering a hopeful perspective on human nature and societal development. The story is focused, detailed, and has broad significance for understanding humanity's past and the foundations of culture and technology.
articles.personality_title: Ancient containers: a key tool for human survival 500,000 years ago
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Archaeologists have long studied early human tools, often focusing on weapons and stone tools used for hunting and cutting. However, many tools made from wood and other materials have not survived well, leaving gaps in our understanding of early human life.

**What happened** – A team of researchers led by palaeoanthropologist Marc Kissel created a large database of prehistoric containers. These include objects like slings, ostrich eggshells, wooden trays, bone tubes, and stone lamps that were used to hold or carry things. Their study, published in April 2024, shows that containers date back at least 500,000 years, much earlier than the previously believed 10,000 years linked to farming and pottery.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how we think about early human tools. Containers helped our ancestors carry water, food, and even babies, supporting survival and cooperation. Unlike weapons, containers focus on nurturing and sharing, highlighting a different side of human nature. It also shows that early humans used a variety of materials and techniques, not just stone tools, to solve everyday problems.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to explore ancient sites and study old findings with new methods to find more evidence of early containers. This could reveal even older examples and deepen our understanding of how humans developed culture and technology gradually over hundreds of thousands of years.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Containers, simple tools for holding and carrying, were invented by humans at least 500,000 years ago and played a vital role in cooperation and survival long before farming began.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les premiers contenants humains : un outil clé vieux de 500 000 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les archéologues ont souvent étudié les premiers outils humains en se concentrant sur les armes et les outils en pierre utilisés pour la chasse et la coupe. Cependant, de nombreux outils en bois et autres matériaux sont moins bien conservés, ce qui laisse des lacunes dans notre compréhension de la vie des premiers humains.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs dirigée par le paléoanthropologue Marc Kissel a créé une grande base de données sur les contenants préhistoriques. Cela inclut des objets comme des liens, des coquilles d’œufs d’autruche, des plateaux en bois, des tubes en os et des lampes en pierre utilisés pour contenir ou transporter des choses. Leur étude, publiée en avril 2024, montre que les contenants datent d’au moins 500 000 ans, bien plus tôt que les 10 000 ans auparavant estimés, liés à l’agriculture et à la poterie.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre vision des premiers outils humains. Les contenants ont aidé nos ancêtres à transporter de l’eau, de la nourriture et même des bébés, soutenant la survie et la coopération. Contrairement aux armes, les contenants montrent un aspect plus nourricier et partageur de la nature humaine. Ils montrent aussi que les premiers humains utilisaient divers matériaux et techniques, pas seulement la pierre, pour résoudre leurs problèmes quotidiens.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à explorer des sites anciens et à étudier les découvertes anciennes avec de nouvelles méthodes pour trouver plus de preuves de contenants anciens. Cela pourrait révéler des exemples encore plus vieux et approfondir notre compréhension du développement progressif de la culture et de la technologie humaines.

**En résumé** – Les contenants, outils simples pour tenir et transporter, ont été inventés par les humains il y a au moins 500 000 ans et ont joué un rôle vital dans la coopération et la survie bien avant le début de l’agriculture.
articles.personality_title_es: Los primeros recipientes humanos: una herramienta clave de hace 500,000 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los arqueólogos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo las primeras herramientas humanas, centrándose a menudo en armas y herramientas de piedra usadas para cazar y cortar. Sin embargo, muchas herramientas hechas de madera y otros materiales no se han conservado bien, dejando vacíos en nuestro conocimiento sobre la vida de los primeros humanos.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores dirigido por el paleoantropólogo Marc Kissel creó una gran base de datos sobre recipientes prehistóricos. Estos incluyen objetos como slings, cáscaras de huevo de avestruz, bandejas de madera, tubos de hueso y lámparas de piedra que se usaban para contener o transportar cosas. Su estudio, publicado en abril de 2024, muestra que los recipientes datan de al menos 500,000 años, mucho antes de los 10,000 años que se creía anteriormente, relacionados con la agricultura y la cerámica.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia la forma en que pensamos sobre las primeras herramientas humanas. Los recipientes ayudaron a nuestros antepasados a transportar agua, comida e incluso bebés, apoyando la supervivencia y la cooperación. A diferencia de las armas, los recipientes muestran un lado más cuidadoso y compartidor de la naturaleza humana. También muestra que los primeros humanos usaban una variedad de materiales y técnicas, no solo herramientas de piedra, para resolver problemas diarios.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán explorando sitios antiguos y estudiando hallazgos antiguos con nuevos métodos para encontrar más evidencia de recipientes antiguos. Esto podría revelar ejemplos aún más antiguos y profundizar nuestra comprensión sobre cómo los humanos desarrollaron la cultura y la tecnología gradualmente durante cientos de miles de años.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los recipientes, herramientas simples para contener y transportar, fueron inventados por humanos hace al menos 500,000 años y jugaron un papel vital en la cooperación y la supervivencia mucho antes de que comenzara la agricultura.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gently glowing ostrich eggshell container resting on a rustic wooden tray beside a soft, woven sling cradle, set against a muted earth-toned background that evokes ancient times and human care, symbolizing early tools of survival and nurturing without showing any human figures.
